does: This paper develops a system for identifying and reducing hallucinations in large vision-language models at the sentence level rather than coarsely across entire outputs. The authors create a fine-grained hallucination detection dataset using proprietary models to label three primary hallucination types—object, attribute, and relationship—then train a detection model on this data. Building on these detections, they implement a detect-then-rewrite pipeline that automatically generates preference pairs for training a hallucination mitigation model. The approach also incorporates severity differentiation among hallucinations, enabling more targeted correction strategies that improve alignment between generated text and visual content.
connects: This work extends prior research on hallucination in language and multimodal models by moving beyond coarse-grained detection methods toward fine-grained, sentence-level analysis. It responds to the limitations of existing approaches that either rely on expensive human annotation or proprietary model labels, building instead on techniques for generating training data through AI feedback. The paper engages with established lines of work on preference learning and reward modeling for aligning model outputs, adapting these methods to the specific challenge of vision-language grounding. The taxonomy of hallucination types draws on earlier characterizations of factual errors in multimodal generation while introducing structured classification for detection and mitigation.
enables: This approach enables more practical and scalable hallucination correction in deployed vision-language systems by reducing annotation burden through automated feedback generation. The fine-grained detection framework creates opportunities for real-time hallucination identification in production settings and supports downstream applications requiring high factual accuracy, such as medical imaging analysis or visual question answering. The severity-aware mitigation strategy opens directions for adaptive correction that considers context-dependent hallucination impacts. Future work can build on this foundation to develop interactive refinement loops where users guide model correction, extend the framework to emerging multimodal architectures, and investigate whether hallucination patterns transfer across different vision-language model families.
